---
title: Zsh Profiles
subtitle: "Customizing Z Shell (zsh) terminals: Mac OS Catalina and later (>10.15)"
---

::: {.callout-important}
Z shell is currently only used in Mac OS. The information on this page is not applicable to Linux or Windows. See [Bash Profiles](bash.qmd) for customizing Linux and GitBash (Windows) terminals.
:::

The appearance and behavior of Z shell terminal windows can be configured using a series of hidden files. It is best practice to take a module approach using several separate files, all of which must be located in the user home directory (`~/`), and each of which has a unique purpose:

1. `~/.zprofile`: Loaded only at login. Used for things that should only run once per login session — environment variables, PATH setup that doesn't need to run in every subshell, *etc.*
2. `~/.zshrc`: Loaded for every interactive shell on Mac OS. Used for PATH setup, prompt configuration, *etc.*
3. `~/.aliases`: An optional file to hold user shortcut aliases.  

## My configurations

```{.zsh filename="~/.zprofile"}
# PATHs
path=("/opt/homebrew/bin" $path)
path+=("$HOME/bin")
```

<br>

```{.zsh filename="~/.zshrc"}
# PATHs (additions beyond the macOS system default)
path+=(
  "/opt/podman/bin"
  "/Applications/quarto/bin"
  "/Library/TeX/texbin"
)

# Case-insensitive auto-complete
zstyle ':completion:*' matcher-list '' 'm:{a-zA-Z}={A-Za-z}' 'r:|=*' 'l:|=* r:|=*'
autoload -Uz compinit && compinit

# GIT
setopt prompt_subst

# VENV — shows active python venv, or non-base conda env; omitted otherwise
venv_prompt_info() {
  local env=""
  if [[ -n $VIRTUAL_ENV ]]; then
    env="$(basename "$VIRTUAL_ENV")"
  elif [[ -n $CONDA_DEFAULT_ENV && $CONDA_DEFAULT_ENV != "base" ]]; then
    env="$CONDA_DEFAULT_ENV"
  fi
  [[ -z $env ]] && return
  print -n "%F{white}(%f%F{cyan}${env}%f%F{white})%f "
}

# GIT — shows branch + dirty marker; omitted if not in a repo
git_prompt_info() {
  local branch
  branch="$(git symbolic-ref --short HEAD 2>/dev/null)" || branch="$(git rev-parse --short HEAD 2>/dev/null)"
  [[ -z $branch ]] && return
  local dirty=""
  [[ -n $(git status --porcelain 2>/dev/null) ]] && dirty="%F{red}*%f"
  print -n "%F{white}[%f%F{green}${branch}%f${dirty}%F{white}]%f "
}

# USER@HOST — shown only when connected via SSH
ssh_prompt_info() {
  [[ -n $SSH_CONNECTION ]] || return
  print -n "%F{magenta}%n@%m%f "
}

# PROMPT
export VIRTUAL_ENV_DISABLE_PROMPT=1
PROMPT='%(?,,%F{red}✗ %f)$(ssh_prompt_info)$(venv_prompt_info)%F{yellow}%1~/%f $(git_prompt_info)%F{white}$ %f'

# ALIASES (shared across platforms)
[[ -f ~/.aliases ]] && source ~/.aliases
```

<br>

```{.bash filename=".aliases"}
# LIST BEHAVIOR
# Cross-Platform 'ls' configuration
if [[ "$OSTYPE" == "darwin"* ]]; then
    # macOS flags: -F for indicators, -G for color
    alias ls="ls -FG"
else
    # Windows/Linux (GNU) flags: -F for indicators, --color=auto for color
    alias ls="ls -F --color=auto"
fi
alias ll="ls -lh"
alias la="ls -lha"

# INTERACTIVE REMOVE
alias rm="rm -i"
alias mv="mv -i"

# NAVIGATION
alias ..="cd .."
alias ...="cd ../.."
alias ....="cd ../../.."
alias .....="cd ../../../.."

# COMMON DIRECTORIES
alias dc="cd ~/Documents"
alias dl="cd ~/Downloads"
alias dt="cd ~/Desktop"
alias home="cd ~"

# GITHUB SHORTCUTS
alias gs="git status"
alias gc="git commit -m"
```

## Discussion

In the setup above, `.zshrc` configures the terminal prompt to show `(pyvenv) pwd.basename [git-branch] $ `, where

* `(pyvenv)` is the name of the activated Python virtual environment, if applicable (omitted if not)
* `user@pwd.basename` is the username and the current directory of the present working directory (pwd)
* `[git-branch]` is the checked out Github branch if the present working directory is a Github repository (omitted otherwise)
* ` $ ` is the final character, followed by a space, preceding user prompts.  

If the user is connected to a remote system via SSH, the prompt will show `(pyvenv) user@hostname pwd.basename [git-branch] $ `, where

* `user` is the current user name
* `hostname` is the name of the remote system

The `.aliases` controls, among other things, how files and directories are listed in the terminal:

1. **List behavior:** Shortcuts for detailed lists (`-l`) with human-readable file sizes (`-h) and including hidden files (`-a`). Items are color-coded and marked depending on type (*e.g.*,directory, file, executable file) for clarity. Some differences exist between platforms, so an operating system (OS) check is used to make the file itself OS-agnostic.
2. **Interactive remove:** Always ask before deleting (`rm`) or overwriting (`mv`, if the file already exists), a safeguard against accidentally removing files
3. **Navigation:** Shortcuts for moving up the directory chain
4. **Common directories:** Shortcuts for moving to commonly-used directories
5. **GitHub Shortcuts:** Shortcuts for commonly-used Git commands

## After updating any of these files

After updating any these files, either restart the terminal or use `source <filename>` to load the new settings.

## Understanding the Terminal Prompt

### `PROMPT` components

| Code | Description                                                          |
|:----:|:---------------------------------------------------------------------|
`%(?,,%F{red}✗ %f)` | `%(x,true-text,false-text)` is zsh's ternary prompt syntax. `?` is the test condition shorthand for "did the last command exit with status 0 (success)?" The true branch (success) is empty — nothing between the first two commas. The false branch (failure) is `%F{red}✗ %f` — a red `✗` followed by a space, then `%f` resets color. Net effect: shows a red `✗` only after a failing command; invisible otherwise.
`$(ssh_prompt_info)` | Runs the `ssh_prompt_info` function, which returns `username@hostname` (magenta) only if `$SSH_CONNECTION` is set, otherwise prints nothing.
`$(venv_prompt_info)` | Runs the function `venv_prompt_info`, inserts `(envname)` in white parens/cyan name if a virtual environment or non-base Conda environment is active; nothing otherwise.
`%F{yellow}%1~/%f` | Displays the current directory. `%F{yellow}` starts yellow text; `%f` (lowercase) resets to default color afterward. `%1~` is zsh's "show the last 1 path component, using `~` shorthand for home" — *e.g.*, `/Users/username/Documents/project` shows as just `project/`, and the home directory itself shows as `~/`. (The number controls how many trailing path segments to show — `%2~/` would show `Documents/project/`, *etc.*)
`$(git_prompt_info)` | Runs the function `git_prompt_info`, inserts`[branch]` (white brackets, green branch, red `*` if dirty) plus a trailing space, or nothing if not in a repo.
`%F{white}$ %f` | Sets the prompt character: white `$` followed by a space, then reset.
: Breakdown of the zsh prompt

### Colors and variables

* `%F{colorname}` / `%f` — start/stop foreground color (there's also `%K{}`/`%k` for background color, unused here).
* `%~`, `%1~`, `%2~`, *etc.* — directory display with varying numbers of trailing segments shown.
* `%n` / `%m` — username / hostname (used inside `ssh_prompt_info`, not directly in the main `PROMPT` line).
* `$(...)` versus backticks — command substitution; either works in zsh, `$(...)` is just the modern/preferred form and nests more cleanly.

## After updating any of these files

After updating any these files, either restart the terminal or use `source <filename>` to load the new settings.
